---
title: "LeetCode 155. Min Stack"
subtitle: "Min Stack"
lang: en
date: 2026-07-16
author: Anthony
categories: [LeetCode, Array, Stack]
description: "[Problem link](https://leetcode.com/problems/min-stack/description/)"
image: ../../cover/Leetcode.svg
alternate-lang: "/dsa/posts/leetcode/0155-minstack/0155-minstack.html"
---

Besides the basic `push`, `pop`, and `top`, this problem asks for a data structure that can also retrieve the minimum value in the stack in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time.

## Approach

Without reading the problem carefully, I happily wrote the following code first:

```python
class MinStack:

    def __init__(self):
        self._stack = []

    def push(self, value: int) -> None:
        self._stack.append(value)

    def pop(self) -> None:
        self._stack.pop()

    def top(self) -> int:
        if not self._stack:
            return 0
        return self._stack[-1]

    def getMin(self) -> int:
        return min(self._stack)
```

Hit run feeling pretty good about it. Then: [**Time Limit Exceeded**]{style="color: red;"}.

![](images/argh.gif){fig-align="center"}

Looking more carefully at the problem, every function is required to run in constant time.

The first few functions aren't a problem, since pushing, popping, and peeking a stack are all already $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The tricky one is getting the minimum value — scanning the whole stack to find it takes $\mathcal{O}(n)$, and that's where I got stuck.

But wait — since this is a stack, why not just lean into what a stack is already good at: constant-time push, pop, and peek? Every time a value is pushed, compare it against the current known minimum, and push whichever is smaller onto a second, auxiliary stack. The top of that auxiliary stack then always holds **the current minimum of the main stack** — which is presumably why it's called `minStack` (the problem's naming was more of a hint than I gave it credit for).

In other words, every level of the auxiliary stack stores the minimum value at the moment that level was pushed. The two stacks always stay the same length, one-to-one. With that, all four operations run in constant time, at the cost of an auxiliary stack as long as the main one — a textbook trade of space for time.

## Implementation

::: {.panel-tabset}
## C

```c
typedef struct
{
    int *_stack;
    int *_minStack; // shadow stack: running minimum at each depth
    int count;
} MinStack;

MinStack *minStackCreate()
{
    MinStack *obj = (MinStack *)malloc(sizeof(MinStack));
    obj->count = 0;
    obj->_stack = (int *)malloc(30000 * sizeof(int));
    obj->_minStack = (int *)malloc(30000 * sizeof(int));
    return obj;
}

void minStackPush(MinStack *obj, int value)
{
    obj->_stack[obj->count] = value;

    // Push the smaller of the new value and the current minimum
    if (obj->count == 0)
        obj->_minStack[obj->count] = value;
    else
        obj->_minStack[obj->count] = value < obj->_minStack[obj->count - 1] ? value : obj->_minStack[obj->count - 1];

    obj->count++;
}

void minStackPop(MinStack *obj)
{
    // Both stacks shrink together
    obj->count--;
}

int minStackTop(MinStack *obj)
{
    return obj->_stack[obj->count - 1];
}

int minStackGetMin(MinStack *obj)
{
    return obj->_minStack[obj->count - 1];
}

void minStackFree(MinStack *obj)
{
    free(obj->_stack);
    free(obj->_minStack);
    free(obj);
}
```

## C++

```cpp
class MinStack
{
private:
    vector<int> _stack;
    vector<int> _minStack; // shadow stack: running minimum at each depth

public:
    MinStack()
    {
    }

    void push(int value)
    {
        _stack.push_back(value);

        // Push the smaller of the new value and the current minimum
        if (_minStack.empty())
            _minStack.push_back(value);
        else
        {
            int minValue = min(value, _minStack.back());
            _minStack.push_back(minValue);
        }
    }

    void pop()
    {
        // Both stacks shrink together
        _stack.pop_back();
        _minStack.pop_back();
    }

    int top()
    {
        return _stack.back();
    }

    int getMin()
    {
        return _minStack.back();
    }
};
```

## Python

```python
class MinStack:

    def __init__(self):
        self._stack = []
        self._minStack = []  # shadow stack: running minimum at each depth

    def push(self, value: int) -> None:
        self._stack.append(value)

        # Push the smaller of the new value and the current minimum
        if not self._minStack:
            self._minStack.append(value)
            return
        min_value = min(value, self._minStack[-1])
        self._minStack.append(min_value)
        return

    def pop(self) -> None:
        # Both stacks shrink together
        self._stack.pop()
        self._minStack.pop()

    def top(self) -> int:
        if not self._stack:
            return 0
        return self._stack[-1]

    def getMin(self) -> int:
        return self._minStack[-1]
```
:::
